text: Chrysippus, the third head of the Stoa and the philosopher most responsible for systematizing Stoic doctrine, described passion (pathos) as a false judgment — not a force that overwhelms reason, but a mistaken belief about value. When we are distracted by novelty or excitement, we are not being overpowered; we are agreeing, however briefly, that the distraction matters more than what we were doing. This makes distraction a philosophical error, not merely a neurological one. The remedy Chrysippus prescribed was not willpower but clarity: correct the underlying belief, and the pull of the distraction dissolves.
question: How did Chrysippus define passion (pathos)?

options.text: A force that overwhelms reason and demands to be released
A false judgment — a mistaken belief about value
A natural appetite that must be balanced, not eliminated
An imbalance of the four bodily humors
